request.question: The soundtrack to 'Dancer in the Dark' is by what Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ?

title: Björk
Where Is the Line
Dancer in the Dark
List of songs recorded by Björk
I've Seen It All
Jófríður Ákadóttir
Greta Salóme Stefánsdóttir
Innocence (Björk song)
Michael Flessas
Selmasongs
sentences: Björk Guðmundsdóttir (] , born 21 November 1965), known as Björk ( ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ.

Over her four-decade career, she has developed an eclectic musical style that draws on a wide range of influences and genres spanning electronic, pop, experimental, classical, trip hop, IDM, and avant-garde styles.
She initially became known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band The Sugarcubes, whose 1987 single "Birthday" was a hit on US and UK indie stations and a favorite among music critics.

Björk embarked on a solo career in 1993, coming to prominence as a solo artist with albums such as "Debut" (1993), "Post" (1995), and "Homogenic" (1997), while collaborating with a range of artists and exploring a variety of multimedia projects.
"Where Is the Line" is a song recorded by Icelandic singer Björk for her sixth studio album "Medúlla".
It was written by the singer herself and co-produced with longtime collaborator Mark Bell.

The track was originally intended to be released as the album's third commercial single, but its release was cancelled just before Björk released the "Drawing Restraint 9" soundtrack, in order to focus all promotional efforts on the film and soundtrack instead.
Some promotional white labels vinyls were released around 2005.
A music video for the song was made by Icelandic Artist Gabríela Friðriksdóttir.
Upon the cancellation of the single, the video was used as part of a video installation project by Friðriksdóttir.
Dancer in the Dark (Danish: Danser i mørket) is a 2000 Danish musical drama film directed by Lars von Trier.

It stars Icelandic singer Björk as a daydreaming immigrant factory worker who suffers from a degenerative eye condition and is saving up to pay for an operation to prevent her young son from suffering the same fate.
Catherine Deneuve, David Morse, Cara Seymour, Peter Stormare, Siobhan Fallon Hogan, and Joel Grey also star.
Icelandic singer and songwriter Björk has recorded more than one hundred songs for eight studio albums, two soundtrack albums, a compilation album, five remix albums and two collaboration albums.
She is the sole writer and producer of most of the songs included in her albums.
She also sometimes plays instruments during her recording sessions.
Moreover, she has provided credited and uncredited contributions on songs recorded by other artists, including background vocals, songwriting, remixing and production.
"I've Seen It All" is a song by Icelandic singer Björk, with lyrics by Sjón and Lars von Trier.
It was released as the first promotional single from the "Dancer in the Dark" soundtrack, "".
The song features vocals from Radiohead frontman Thom Yorke.
It was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Original Song.
Lyrically, it speaks of one coming to terms with the fact that they are going blind.
Jófríður Ákadóttir, known professionally as JFDR, is an Icelandic singer, songwriter and multi-instrumentalist born in 1994.
She is known for her solo project JFDR as well as her work with Samaris, Pascal Pinon and Gangly and works closely with producer and multi-instrumentalist .
Her father is Icelandic composer and musician Áki Ásgeirsson.
Greta Salóme Stefánsdóttir (] ; born 11 November 1986), known outside Iceland simply as Greta Salóme (] ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, and violinist in the Icelandic Symphony Orchestra.
She represented Iceland in the Eurovision Song Contest 2012 in Baku, Azerbaijan, in a duet with singer Jónsi with the song "Never Forget".
They qualified from the first semi-final into the Eurovision final and eventually finished in 20th place with 46 points.
She represented Iceland again, this time as a soloist, in the Eurovision Song Contest 2016 with the song "Hear Them Calling", where she failed to qualify.
"Innocence" is a song written and recorded by Icelandic singer Björk.
The song was released as the second single from her seventh full-length studio album, "Volta".
It is co-produced by record producer Timbaland and his protégé Danja.
The song was premiered on Björk's MySpace profile on March 19, 2007 as part of the fan contest to submit ideas for the music video.
The single was released exclusively as a digital download on July 23, 2007.
Michael C. Flessas (born June 2, 1959 in Miami, Florida), is the birth name of American actor Michael Flessas, who is of Greek ancestry.
Flessas' most notable film role was "Angry Man" in the Cannes Film Festival 2000 Palme d'Or winning film "Dancer in the Dark" directed by Danish film director Lars von Trier.
Originally, the director himself considered playing the role but, instead, the role was given to Flessas.
"Dancer in the Dark" starred Icelandic singer/actress Björk who won the Best Actress award at Cannes for her role.

French film icon, César Award winner, and Academy Award nominee Catherine Deneuve, and other noteworthy artists such as Academy Award and Tony Award winner Joel Grey, Peter Stormare, David Morse, and Stellan Skarsgård also performed in the multiple prize winning film.
One of Björk's songs for the film received an Academy Award nomination for Best Song.
Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.
It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".
In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.
The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: The soundtrack to 'Dancer in the Dark' is by what Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ?
hop1_docs: Mary J. Blige | Mary Jane Blige ( ; born January 11, 1971) is an American singer, songwriter, rapper, model, record producer and actress.  Starting her career as a backing singer on Uptown Records in 1989, Blige released her first album, "What's the 411? ", in 1992, and has released 13 studio albums since and made over 150 guest appearances on other albums and soundtracks.

Selmasongs | Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.  It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".  In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.  The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

Samurai Champloo Music Record: Departure | Samurai Champloo Music Record: Departure is a soundtrack album, released on June 23, 2004, for the anime series "Samurai Champloo".  It is the second of four soundtrack albums released for the show.  The album was produced by Japanese DJ/producer Nujabes, and American MC/producer Fat Jon, and featured Japanese rapper Shing02 and Japanese singer-songwriter MINMI on the album's first and last tracks, respectively.
List of awards and nominations received by Anahí | Anahí is a Mexican singer-songwriter, actress, record producer and dancer.

Vespertine | Vespertine is the fourth solo album by Icelandic musician Björk, released on 27 August 2001, on One Little Indian Records.  It was recorded at locations in Spain, Iceland, and the United States in 2000; production began during the filming of "Dancer in the Dark", which was characterized by conflict between the singer and director Lars von Trier. " Björk", a self-titled coffee table book containing photographs of the singer throughout her career, was released simultaneously with the album.  "Vespertine"' s sound reflected Björk's newly found interest in the music of artists such as Opiate and Console, who were also enlisted as producers.
Charlotte Devaney | Charlotte Devaney is a British DJ, producer, singer, dancer and actress.

Howard Donald | Howard Paul Donald (born 28 April 1968), is an English singer, songwriter, drummer, pianist, dancer, DJ and record producer.  He is a member of English pop-group Take That and a judge on the German reality talent show "Got to Dance".

response.summary_1:

1) Evidence found:
- Selmasongs (passage [2]) — quotes: "first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk." (passage [2])
- Vespertine (passage [5]) — quotes: "production began during the filming of 'Dancer in the Dark'." (passage [5])

2) Ambiguities:
- The question's occupation string ("singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ") is not explicitly listed in the passages. Passages only refer to "Icelandic musician Björk." 
- Possible candidate pages implied: "Selmasongs" (album) and "Björk" (artist). The passages do not show the full personal name variant "Björk Guðmundsdóttir" or a dedicated film soundtrack credits section.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as in the passages): "Björk"
- Precise missing fact needed: confirmation that Björk is credited as the artist/composer of the Dancer in the Dark soundtrack and that her occupations explicitly include "singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ" (or which of those occupations are officially listed).

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Fetch the "Selmasongs" page (open lede/infobox and "Credits"/"Personnel"/Track listing) to verify soundtrack artist and composer/producer credits. Search keywords: Selmasongs credits Björk "soundtrack" "artist".
- Fetch the "Björk" main page (open lede/infobox and "Occupation" and "Filmography"/"Discography" sections) to confirm the exact occupational list (actress, record producer, DJ). Search keywords: Björk occupations actress DJ record producer.
- If needed, fetch "Dancer in the Dark (film)" page and check the "Music" or "Soundtrack" section for soundtrack attribution.

Do not produce the final answer yet — retrieve the above pages/sections to confirm the missing facts before concluding.
hop2_docs: Selmasongs | Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.  It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".  In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.  The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

Vespertine World Tour | The Vespertine World Tour was a tour by the singer Björk that focused on her album "Vespertine".  She also performed songs from "Debut", "Post", "Homogenic" and "Selmasongs".  She performed 35 shows on this tour and 31 different songs.  The tour band consisted of harpist Zeena Parkins, electronic duo Matmos, a Greenlandic choir and a 70-piece orchestra (local to each venue).  The tour was released on DVD as "Live at Royal Opera House" and the concert held at the Orchard Hall in Tokyo, Japan was broadcast on Japanese TV-station "WOWOW".  A separate release, "Vespertine Live", contains a selection of Björk's favourite performances from throughout the Vespertine tour, while a tour documentary, "Minuscule", was also released.

Where Is the Line | "Where Is the Line" is a song recorded by Icelandic singer Björk for her sixth studio album "Medúlla".  It was written by the singer herself and co-produced with longtime collaborator Mark Bell.  The track was originally intended to be released as the album's third commercial single, but its release was cancelled just before Björk released the "Drawing Restraint 9" soundtrack, in order to focus all promotional efforts on the film and soundtrack instead.  Some promotional white labels vinyls were released around 2005.  A music video for the song was made by Icelandic Artist Gabríela Friðriksdóttir.  Upon the cancellation of the single, the video was used as part of a video installation project by Friðriksdóttir.

Sucker Punch (soundtrack) | Sucker Punch is the soundtrack for the film of the same name.  It was released on March 22, 2011 by WaterTower Music.  The album consists of covered songs, mainly by the film's stars.  Emily Browning sings three songs, and Carla Gugino and Oscar Isaac performs a cover of "Love Is the Drug" as a duet.  Recording artists Björk, Skunk Anansie, Emilíana Torrini, Queen, Carla Azar, Alison Mosshart and Yoav also have songs on the soundtrack.

List of songs recorded by Björk | Icelandic singer and songwriter Björk has recorded more than one hundred songs for eight studio albums, two soundtrack albums, a compilation album, five remix albums and two collaboration albums.  She is the sole writer and producer of most of the songs included in her albums.  She also sometimes plays instruments during her recording sessions.  Moreover, she has provided credited and uncredited contributions on songs recorded by other artists, including background vocals, songwriting, remixing and production.

Enjoyed: A Tribute to Björk's Post | Enjoyed: A Tribute to Björk's Post, sometimes referred to as Stereogum Presents... Enjoyed: A Tribute to Björk's Post, is a tribute album by various artists, released by Stereogum in 2008.  The compilation album contains cover versions of all eleven tracks from Björk's album "Post", originally released in 1995, plus an alternate take of "It's Oh So Quiet".

Virus (Björk song) | "Virus" is a song by Icelandic artist Björk released as the third single from the album "Biophilia".  Each song in the album features a theme related to nature.  In "Virus", Björk explores "fatal relationships" such as the one between a virus and a cell, as Björk explained in an interview: "It's a kind of a love story between a virus and a cell.  And of course the virus loves the cell so much that it destroys it."

response.summary_2: Selmasongs is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk. Björk is an Icelandic singer and songwriter who starred as Selma Ježková in the film and has recorded more than one hundred songs.
